item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
5031 is a good boy, and deserves all the chicken.

Thank you for writing this. In the current climate of lockdown and isolation, this genuinely made me smile.

+1

item.title: yay
item.content:encoded: I LOVE THIS ONE!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my new fav scp, made me actually smile when SCP-5031 learned to spell or make its first word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obviously that doesn't happen.

Why do you think it doesn't happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ah but would the chickens not make him disappear just as humans do when they look at him

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite tales ever. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-5031 has said its first word: "Salt".

We are all immensely proud.

Sir, I protest, this is illegal. ;_;

This is somehow both the most heartwarming shit and the most scathing indictment of 'classic' Foundation I have ever seen. +1 isn't enough reward for this article.

Also, I highly approve of the use of Ben Folds to pacify murder monsters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
on a bed of kale

Put it back in the box, it's irredeemable.

For real though I think this is one of my favourite articles on the site.

item.title: There’s Some Fridge Horror Here
item.content:encoded:
Obviously I love the baby boy myself as well, but honestly what I love most about this is that there’s actually some rather troubling implications from this interaction.

The whole concept hinges on the idea that the first article written by the previous researcher was laughably inaccurate at describing the complexity of the creatures mentality and threat. It was because of this bad documentation that the thing was chucked into a box for ages and allowed to suffer for no reason. I would go so far as to say that the whole “attacks and consumes all creatures it encounters” is a straight up farce. It probably just hunted and killed things occasionally because despite not requiring sustenance it still seems to experience hunger and it just didn’t know any better than to attack humans. It had the mentality of a toddler after all. The whole first article is a very clever way at poking fun at the melodrama and boring simplicity of the generic murder monster trope on the site, but in universe the generic murder monster that must be kept in a box trope is presented here as a real problem within the foundation of researchers not taking the time to fully understand an anomaly beyond its base threats or to consider the well-being of said anomaly.

This begs the question: Just how many inaccurately recorded and unreasonably contained SCPS are there?? How many generic murder monsters on this site are actually products of a biased researcher making snap judgements about complex creatures? There could be any number of sentient beings harbored by the foundation that, with proper treatment, could also learn to become more than a monster in a cage. If an article as incredibly misleading as the one Huxtable contested could go uncontested for a whole decade, it makes you wonder if maybe there are a few other articles leaving out a few key details…

I overall like the concept because it executes it in a way that feels real. There are so many points where this could have gotten cringy cutesie or forced, but it very well balanced the idea of there being an alien and possibly dangerous monster who displays a capacity to learn and has an actual personality. It’s wholesome but not overly indulgent in that fact. So yeah, it makes me feel good reading it. I also just like the idea that this Huxtable guy is part of some kind of containment reform movement that’s gonna uncover a bunch of dumb things the foundation has done over the years. Judging from this article, they sorely need one.

Anyways, TLDR: Very clever way of turning the tropes we’re used to on its head. Could have been a bit more conflict but honestly I’m just glad this thing exists. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this a while ago but only made an account recently. This is one of my favorite things on the site and touches me deeply personally. I work in disability care and so many of the people I support were institutionalized. I don’t know if that was part of the intended metaphor but it’s part of what really hits me about this piece.

(And of course the importance of loving your monsters which always hits me hard.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was amazing.

100,000 / 10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this one. It was great seeing the "Keter" classification, reading through it and seeing all the good things happening all while waiting for that inevitable "Monster became so smart it broke containment and killed all of Site-##". But that never happened… I was bracing myself for it but it never came. That, alone, made it a good read on my end.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking even as I was reading, this SCP better have a high rating.

An amazing job really enjoyable to read.

Would double upvote if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is what you call a redemption story!

But no seriously this is the most adorable shit ever (excluding 999, for obvious reasons). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was very relieved that the D-class didn't accidentally get killed. It seemed pretty clear the thing wouldn't intentionally kill one - its original "horror archetype" clearly was "mindless ravening predator", not "cunning deceiver" - but the first Chicken incident could have been foreshadowing for "some tragic accident happens to the D-class, without malice aforethought" (Something like "nose broken by a tennis ball" to "legs shattered by a bowling ball", I was hoping would be the worst - I would have been disappointed if it had been lethal. "everything went fine" was much more pleasant. Good thing they sent in the chicken first.)

item.title: It does seem that we're seeing more sentient SCPs being treated decently
item.content:encoded:
Dionysus comes to mind, and Hera, and now this. Even Sarkicism, CotBG, and a couple other canons have been humanizing the weirdness and drawing connections, rather than simply throwing things into boxes.

Well I initially came for the horror, and stayed for the history and secret machinations from the dawn of time, watching these stories get fluffier, get slice of lifey, seems really nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: by far my favorite 5000 scp i just find it nice and wholesome

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't begin to express how heartwarming it was to read "We are all immensely proud." An absolutely amazing read, and it deserves every one of its accolades and praise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was wonderful to read. I was absolutely hooked from the get-go, and devoured every new experiment. There was a persistent undertone of sadness and horror that kept building throughout the experiment log. When I got to 'it can learn language, Youssef', I had to scroll up to re-read the original containment document, and honestly wanted to cry when I saw the words 'non-sapient' again. No wonder 5031 was screaming.

You did a fantastic job with this, and that ending was absolutely adorable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Peppers, I haven't sat down and read a new(er) SCP article in a long time, but I figured one of yours would've a safe bet after some discord discussion about it.

And I was right. I often recommend your articles when trying to get people into SCP, and here we have another one I can use to entice new readers. What a joy.

item.title: Favorite SCP
item.content:encoded: This is the only ever SCP to legitimately make me tear up. It’s a wonderful piece. I don’t know why but the part that got me the most was making sure to mark that it has a peanut allergy in the revised containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, I've been doing this for a while, but this really was one of the best I've ever read.

item.title: Revisiting with some more praise.
item.content:encoded: I haven't memorized the number, so I keep coming back to this article by googling "scp cooking monster" — this is the top result of that coveted title. Well-earned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't recall this one being so long, but it is a great one. Instant favorite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had to sign in to disagree with this. I really see where you are coming from, and if this weren’t a flash-fiction/short story, I’d probably side with you. But in this case:

The opening of this story explains that everything you want from it; the bumps, the dead researchers/D-Class, the pitfalls have happened before the story starts. And it’s using the format and our expectations of SCPs to swerve us from the fact that this is the end of those pitfalls. This story wouldn’t affect us the same if those pitfalls weren’t in the backstory and we were so happy that this goodboi got out of trouble and into a happy life it deserved.

TL;DR: The conflict is the backstory; we are starting in media res with a “Mentor with a heart of gold” and if this were a movie, some Good Will Hunting style backsliding would be in order, but in short form, having the mentor save the creature from the torture of the Old Guard is good enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article!
It's not only adorable and cute, this also show how scientific methodology can help in understnading the others and turn them from monsters into safer creatures, if they can be safer.
it really touched me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So far, this is the only thing on this site that has managed to make me cry. Take your +1.

Besides being adorable, I love how there was actual research being done. The researchers genuinely tried to understand its motives and opinions, and they even managed to educate it. The tests actually worked off of previous ones, and didn't involve throwing random things at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an amazingly cute SCP and the ending made me go "awww"
+1 for sure

item.title: My first +1
item.content:encoded: Reading this made my day. Well done!

item.title: Take my +1
item.content:encoded: This is a great wholesome nod to the older super-spooky-monster entries on the site. Take my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Massive +1, when I first read it I have it a -1 because I saw it and thought it was just a crappy stereotypical monster and quit reading, which was a massive mistake on my part. Later, I saw some reddit posts about it and read the whole thing. I quickly retracted my -1 when I read it all the way through. I legitimately had a smile on my face as I read this. This quickly went from one of my least favorite go one of my most favorite SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see the Foundation befriending a creature through sound scientific technique.

this article is an incredibly interesting and heartwarming story that makes excellent use of experiment logs in that they excellently illustrate the creatures behavior and the trust and love that it gains towards people

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's how the world should be saved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ethical felines containment is very important to the foundation nowadays

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a good scp one of my favorite of this series

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy Shit this thing blew up. 600+ upvotes?!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 +1 +1 +1 +1 +1 +1 +1 I love him and I want to keep him

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Joining the "no twist = best twist" group. Great work, I have nothing that has not been said already many times over in these comments.

I love the goodest boi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn 6-armed flying scorpion monster is more civilized than I am.

item.title: OMG
item.content:encoded:
This is an amazing article. It's truly heartwarming, and just goes to show that as cold as the Foundation can be sometimes, they still care about their inmates.

+1

item.title: Foundational Enrichment for Misunderstood Monsters
item.content:encoded:
My heaaaaart.

This was a delightful read and a really fun, good tongue-in-cheek reflection on early Foundation procedures.

+1, with great enthusiasm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been so conditioned to expect stuff like this to go south, but honestly the fact it kept me so interested in this thing's development and satisfied without that expected twist. Such a great SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was riveted the whole time, and the amount of heartwarming sticks with me weeks after I first read it. Excellent work, my friend.

Link to reading:
https://youtu.be/r5kJwf4pGrs‬

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1.
This document made me smile, especially "SCP-5031 has said its first word. 'Salt'. We are immensely proud." and "Personnel response was overwhelmingly positive. SCP-5031 stress level at 0%." I was grinning ear-to-ear by the end; the idea of another "murder monster" essentially just being a baby and steadily growing and maturing is one of my favorite concepts by far. I also love the inclusion of SCP-5031 having a peanut allergy, giving it that hint of humanization. This has been one of the most enticing, wholesome reads for me, thank you for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like a lot of the people commenting already, I kept waiting for the twist. When it spelled out MORE MORE MORE I was like "Yup, here is comes". Was immensely satisfied that it never came.

As someone who loves trope inversion, +1

As a biology teacher who appreciates seeing some sort of actual science being done, +1

As an inner-city teacher who is missing his students in this quarantine, and may or may not have started crying reading about someone treating a stressed out, abandoned, and angry individual with patience and compassionate teaching until it learned and grew into a healthy, happy boy, +1 and my heart

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this one, knew I was going to love it as soon as the new tests started and it quickly proved my feelings right. While it's such a small thing, the fact it has a favorite D-class just really adds a little flavor to the peice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thoughts.
Thoughts.

I feel like the issue isn't that the story has no arc, as it obviously does. There is, as you have said, a clear problem that is gradually resolved over the course of the article, that being the misery of 5031's existence. However, this journey, long as it may be, lacks speedbumps, so to speak. Personally, I find that this results in a resolution (because there certainly is one) that is far less satisfying than it could have been.

Sure, there's no antagonist and no obvious obstacles

This is what does it for me. Because of this, the process doesn't feel like a struggle: it just feels really slow. Showing us 5031 making baby-steps is great, yes. But we never get the joy of seeing it trip over and stand back up triumphantly in any meaningful way. There were three very minor instances of this I found, but they are overcome extremely quickly without lasting impacts.

Subject was killed instantly. SCP-5031's stress levels rose immediately and drastically.

Bowling ball chipped and would no longer roll properly. (Unplanned.)

and

Result: SCP-5031 expressed apparent frustration, hitting the inactive button repeatedly and striking the wall with its tail.

In all of these cases, the "struggles" are just momentary increases in stress, discarded immediately in the next test without time for us to lament them, which makes the eventual success feel less emotional. It doesn't need to be a tragedy. It just needs to be a battle hard-fought.

Of course, this is just my opinion and I accept that you feel differently. This piece definitely has its merits, and I agree wholeheartedly that this is a very streamlined piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's a big part of why I would novote, instead of downvoting. It's an interesting take on a monster that is oh so human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this SCP. Easily one of my new favorites. It's one of the few wholesome SCPs out there, it shows a more human side to the Foundation, and I just love the idea of it. A neglected SCP that the Foundation teaches to read, write, and communicate, that then becomes a master chef and piano player, adored by the on-site staff. It just brings a smile to my face, and I can't wait for tales to be made about it. Take my +1, just take it!

item.title: ...dammit, I have to upvote this.
item.content:encoded:
Initially I was feeling like "oh, this is portraying the foundation as too soft", but honestly I think the continued ethical/structural development of the Foundation is an interesting macro-arc in itself.

And…as an autistic person, I'm…really relating to this. A LOT. Are the ever-so-tragic "victims of autism" just "failing" because everyone and everything around them is acting as though "failure" is the only possibility?

Good angle. Definitely worth exploring more.

item.title: Awesome
item.content:encoded:
I really wish more SCPs would be documented in a similar fashion. The foundation being a bunch of super smart scientists with unlimited resources, you'd think they'd run more tests like this for every entity or phenomenon they're aware of.

I'd love to hear more about how they discovered and initially contained this one, it's origin, etc. And I think testing should go even further and consider other aspects, like how it turns invisible when observed. And since it's able to communicate, they should be all over getting all the information they can out of it. But maybe someone can expand on that in the future somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this article, and it is by far one of my favorites on the website. Very few articles here are wholesome, very few have this level of character development, and this is the perfect combo of both. In my opinion, it could be a bit longer, with a less abrupt ending, but the content within was more than satisfactory. We need more of these articles, obviously with some twists or they will become stale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The other issue for me was that there isn't really any conflict or resolution

wat
Rant time here we come

There is very much a conflict and resolution. Sure, there's no antagonist and no obvious obstacles, but that doesn't mean this lacks a conflict, or resolution, or arc.

The conflict here is pretty straightforward: SCP-5031 is being held unfairly in a barbaric form of containment. The resistance is inherent to the struggles of understanding SCP-5031 and SCP-5031 coming to find what it enjoys. It's a process of discovery, and we're along for the journey. The conflict is inherent in the steps they need to take to uncover the master chef hidden underneath the layers of murder-monster stereotypes.

The resolution, by proxy, is also pretty straightforward. The resolution occurs at the end when we see SCP-5031 treated with humanity and respect. It's the ending of the transformation process.

If this style of story doesn't hit for you, that's fine. Everyone can have their own taste, but that's different from a lack of conflict. What I enjoy most about this piece is how the conflict is so intrinsic in the premise that the entirety of the resolution can happen through an evocative character arc. The character arc is almost one and the same as the resolution, which makes this piece really streamlined.

Or perhaps a continuation of this, where the entity starts suffering from an illness. Make it tragic, where our attachment is exploited in some way, with great risk, or even loss

While peppers already addressed this, I wanted to just prod a little more:

What would this serve? What would this add? We already have a character arc. We are already invested in SCP-5031, and the ending already acts as a form of payoff for that investment. This is like killing a character for the sake of killing a character. If it doesn't fit with the throughline of the piece, why do it?

I write a lot of sad stories. But I don't start with trying to write a sad story. I write stories with character arcs and throughlines that just so happen to be sad. But tragedy and loss isn't inherent to character pieces. What matters is that there's a transformation of the character that feels rewarding. Again, if this transformation didn't feel rewarding, that's fine. Everyone has an opinion and all that. But suggesting that adding tragedy would just suddenly improve the piece is like pairing every meal with milk. Sometimes it works, and sometimes it's going to give you a sour stomach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, you make a good point and I hadn't considered that. I would say that this is about more than how the monster's growth though, it also says something about the ethics of the "just seal it away in a metal box" solution the Foundation is often depicted using.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. Not every happy story has to end with a "Gotcha!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're watching 5031's shadow.

item.title: I love this.
item.content:encoded: But… how are we observing it so specifically? How do we know it picks up the basketball, when we can't look at it, or risk looking at it, without it temporarily ceasing to exist?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Novote, after some considerable deliberation
frick cybersqyd posted my thoughts, exactly, before me

Perhaps this is controversial, but I find this doesn't quite do it for me. It's almost there, and I find this to be an intriguing concept, but the ending is less than satisfying. This is largely focused on the character arc of the shadowy murder monster; its growth is beautiful, albeit uneventful and, perhaps, undeserved. It only goes up, with minimal challenge or struggle. It does not need to have a tragic ending. I think that would destroy this story for me. However, the destination is all the more satisfying to reach when there are hardships along the way. It's hard for me to feel the impact of its final successes when it was pitifully easily achieved. I don't feel like Huxtable was as tireless or devoted as you did while writing this.

I would really adore this if, before reaching its peak, the skip had to go through some kind of external challenge. The road to redemption is best when it is bumpy, although that need not be by any fault of its own. Intervention could come from any source, be that a containment breach of some sort, an injury that impeded on progress, an ethical dilemma to display the culmination of its change. An opportunity to cast it all away would make it all the more satisfying to see it prevail. Without that, I'm left underwhelmed.
